preamble: The transformation from a voiced retroflex lateral approximant /ɭ/ to a voiced retroflex stop /ɖ/ represents a process of fortition, where the degree of stricture in the vocal tract increases. This involves the complete closure of the vocal tract, converting an approximant into a plosive at the retroflex place of articulation.

examples.note: The retroflex lateral approximant of Vedic Sanskrit was replaced by the corresponding retroflex stop in Classical Sanskrit.
Fortition of /ɭ/ to /ɖ/ occurred as a later innovation, distinguishing northern dialects.

sources: Vedic Sanskrit had a retroflex lateral approximant (/ɭ/) (ळ) as well as its aspirated counterpart /ɭʰ/ (ळ्ह), which were lost in Classical Sanskrit, to be replaced with the corresponding plosives /ɖ/ (ड) and /ɖʰ/ (ढ).
Miyake, M. (2021). Studies in Pyu phonology, I: Onsets. Language and Linguistics, 22(1), 28-70.
In vedic sanskrit, retroflex voiced dentals [ड, ढ][ɖ, ɖh] have lateral allophones [ळ, ऴ][ ɭ, ɭh].
